request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"onlive::gaming","name":"onlive","one_liner":"Cloud-streaming gaming service that let subscribers play PC and console titles without local hardware, killed by infrastructure economics, latency constraints, and acquisition by Sony in 2015.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":2,"rationale":"OnLive monetized via recurring subscription ($9.99–$14.95/mo) and per-title rental fees — a platform-access model. The new pitch is a free-to-play studio monetizing through cosmetics and a battle pass (transaction/IAP model). Both are B2C gaming revenue streams, but the mechanics are fundamentally different: subscription access vs. optional in-game purchases."},"market":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both target the consumer gaming market and both reached iOS/Android (OnLive had Android app from 2011). The new pitch also targets Steam desktop. OnLive was US-geography focused (cloud infrastructure within 1,000 miles of data centers); the new pitch implies global distribution via storefronts. Overlap is in the consumer gamer audience, but OnLive competed in the cloud delivery infrastructure layer while the new pitch competes in the game content layer."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"OnLive went to market as a streaming platform/service requiring hardware partnerships, ISP deals (BT, AT&T), and hardware distribution (MicroConsole). The new pitch distributes natively through app stores and Steam with no infrastructure dependency, targeting organic/storefront discovery. Completely different GTM motion."},"stage_scale":{"score":3,"rationale":"OnLive was a heavily venture-backed infrastructure startup (raised large rounds from AT&T, Warner Bros., Autodesk, Lauder Partners) with massive capital requirements for data centers and proprietary compression hardware. A B2C indie 4X studio shipping across iOS/Android/Steam is orders of magnitude lighter in capital and infrastructure needs."}},"why_similar":"Both are B2C consumer gaming plays that sought multi-platform reach (mobile + desktop) and faced the challenge of capturing consumer spending in a crowded gaming market. OnLive's Android/iOS clients and the new pitch's iOS/Android/Steam distribution share the same end-user audience of gamers willing to pay for entertainment. Both are also subject to platform-holder gatekeeping (app stores, storefront policies).","where_diverged":"The new pitch diverges from OnLive on nearly every structural dimension: (1) Product layer — OnLive was a cloud-streaming infrastructure platform; the new pitch is a first-party game studio shipping a native title. (2) Monetization — OnLive used subscription_recurring; the new pitch uses cosmetics + battle pass (IAP/transaction). (3) Infrastructure dependency — OnLive required proprietary data centers, low-latency broadband, and custom compression chips; the new pitch has none of these. (4) Content ownership — OnLive licensed third-party games; the new pitch owns its own IP. (5) Geography/scale — OnLive was constrained to within 1,000 miles of US data centers at launch; app-store distribution is global from day one.","failure_causes":["infrastructure unit economics unviable at scale","latency and video quality constraints alienated action/strategy game players","broadband penetration insufficient for mass-market adoption at launch","high capital expenditure on proprietary hardware and data centers","licensing dependency on third-party publishers","Microsoft licensing dispute threatened desktop product","sold assets for $4.8M after $1.8B valuation — catastrophic investor dilution"],"lessons_for_input":["Own your IP and distribution stack — OnLive's reliance on third-party game licenses and ISP partnerships created fatal single points of failure; a native studio owning its own title avoids this.","Validate that your core loop works on the target hardware before scaling — OnLive's latency issues were fatal for fast-paced titles; ensure your 4X strategy game's session length and input model are well-suited to mobile constraints.","Keep infrastructure costs variable, not fixed — OnLive burned cash on data centers regardless of user count; use cloud/engine platforms and app-store distribution to keep costs proportional to revenue.","Design monetization around player psychology from day one — cosmetics and battle passes require deep live-ops cadence; build the systems before launch, not after.","Plan for platform-holder risk — OnLive was hurt by Microsoft threatening its desktop product; app stores can similarly de-list or change revenue-share terms, so diversify across iOS, Android, and Steam from launch."]}
